I finally got offered a recording contract from a small label. What are the biggest red flags I should look for before signing? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

Getting a label offer is exciting — but signing a bad deal can set your career back years. Here are the critical red flags:

Red Flag #1 — Perpetual ownership of masters: If the contract says the label owns your masters 'in perpetuity' or 'for the life of copyright' (which means your lifetime + 70 years), that's a problem. Counter: push for reversion after a set period (7-15 years) or upon full recoupment of advances.

Red Flag #2 — Cross-collateralization: This means the label can use profits from one album to recoup losses from another. If Album 1 earns $100,000 but costs $80,000 to recoup, and Album 2 costs $150,000 to recoup, the label can apply Album 1's $20,000 profit to Album 2's deficit. You earn $0 until the combined balance is positive. Counter: negotiate against cross-collateralization or limit it to related releases only.

Red Flag #3 — Excessive option periods: A '1 album + 4 options' deal means the label can lock you in for 5 albums if they choose — but they can DROP you after any album. The options are one-directional. Counter: maximum 1-2 options. Each option should require the label to increase the advance.

Red Flag #4 — Low royalty rate with high recoupable expenses: A 15% royalty rate sounds reasonable until you realize that recording costs, marketing, video production, tour support, AND producer royalties are all recoupable against YOUR 15%. On a $100,000 album, you might not see a royalty check for years. Counter: understand exactly what's recoupable. Push for non-recoupable marketing spend.

Red Flag #5 — No audit clause: A contract without the right to audit the label's books is a major red flag. Counter: insist on an audit clause allowing you to audit once per year at the label's expense if a discrepancy over 10% is found.

Red Flag #6 — Controlled composition clause: This clause caps the mechanical royalty rate the label pays to you as a songwriter (typically at 75% of the statutory rate). It saves the label money at your expense. Counter: delete this clause entirely, or at minimum negotiate for 100% statutory rate.

Red Flag #7 — No marketing commitment: The label owns your music but has no contractual obligation to actually promote it. Your album sits unreleased while you can't release it yourself or go elsewhere. Counter: include a 'use it or lose it' clause — if the label doesn't release the album within 12-18 months of delivery, rights revert.

Red Flag #8 — The label requires your publishing: If the label also wants your publishing rights (your songwriting income), treat this as a separate negotiation. Never bundle master rights and publishing into one deal without understanding the full impact.

The non-negotiable step: Have an ENTERTAINMENT ATTORNEY review any contract before signing. Not a general attorney — an entertainment attorney. Budget $500-$1,500 for a contract review. This is the best money you'll ever spend on your career.

How do I register my song with the Copyright Office? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal"}]

To register a song (composition) with the U.S. Copyright Office: 1) Go to copyright.gov and create an account. 2) Select 'Register a Work' → 'Performing Arts' (Form PA for songs). 3) Upload a deposit (MP3, sheet music, or lyrics). 4) Pay the fee ($45 for online single work registration). 5) Registration is retroactive to the submission date. Note: You own the copyright the moment you create the work — registration is required to sue for statutory damages and attorney's fees if someone infringes. Always register before releasing publicly. ⚖️ This is informational guidance, not legal advice.

An indie label offered me a deal — $20k advance, 70/30 split in my favor, 2 album commitment, and they own the masters for 7 years. Is this good? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true,"artistTier":"mid-indie"}]

Let me analyze each term:

**$20k Advance — Moderate.** Typical for emerging indie-label deals in 2026. Remember: an advance is a loan recouped from YOUR royalty share. At 70/30 with a $0.004/stream blended rate, your per-stream earn is $0.0028. You need ~7.14 million streams to recoup.

**70/30 Split — Fair for Indie.** Standard for 2026 indie labels. BUT verify: is this on net or gross? Net after distribution fees and marketing costs can effectively make this 50/50.

**2-Album Commitment — CAUTION.** Locks you in for potentially 3-5 years. Negotiate: add a performance trigger (if Album 1 doesn't hit X streams in 12 months, you can exit), and cap the delivery timeline.

**Masters Reversion After 7 Years — NEGOTIATE DOWN.** 7 years is long for an indie deal in 2026. Push for 3-5 years. Your masters are your most valuable long-term asset. If they won't budge, negotiate a reversion trigger (masters revert if the advance is fully recouped, regardless of timeline).

**Missing Terms:** Marketing commitment (get a minimum spend in writing), creative control, distribution transparency (who is their distributor?), cross-collateralization (RED FLAG if present).

**Assessment:** Mediocre as offered. If you negotiate master reversion to 3-5 years, add a marketing floor, eliminate cross-collateralization, and add a performance exit clause — it becomes solid. If they won't negotiate, you can likely do better staying independent.

I used an AI tool to make my voice sound like a famous singer on a track. Can I release this or will I get sued? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

This is one of the most active legal areas in 2026, and the answer is: you are at significant legal risk. Here's the landscape:

The legal risks of AI voice cloning:

(1) Right of Publicity: every US state has some form of right of publicity law that protects a person's name, image, likeness, and in many states, VOICE from unauthorized commercial use. Using an AI to replicate a recognizable singer's voice for commercial release almost certainly violates this right. Tennessee's ELVIS Act (Ensuring Likeness Voice and Image Security Act, 2024) specifically addresses AI-generated voice clones — it's a civil violation to use AI to replicate someone's voice without authorization.

(2) Section 43(a) of the Lanham Act (Federal trademark law): if your use of an AI-cloned voice could confuse consumers into thinking the original artist performed on or endorsed your track, you face federal unfair competition claims.

(3) Label/publisher claims: major labels have aggressively pursued takedowns and legal action against AI-generated tracks using their artists' voices. Universal Music Group's takedown of the viral AI-generated 'Heart on My Sleeve' (fake Drake/The Weeknd) set the precedent.

(4) Platform enforcement: Spotify, Apple Music, and YouTube have all updated their policies to prohibit or restrict AI-generated content that impersonates real artists. Your track could be removed, your account flagged, or your distributor could drop you.

What you CAN do with AI vocals:
(1) Clone YOUR OWN voice: training an AI on your own voice is legal and increasingly common. You can create harmonies, backing vocals, or experimental versions of yourself.
(2) Use AI voices that don't replicate a specific real person: generic AI vocal models that don't sound like any identifiable artist are generally safe.
(3) Licensed AI voice models: some artists have officially licensed their AI voice models (Grimes, Holly Herndon). Using these WITH the terms of their license is legal.
(4) Parody: parody may have First Amendment protection under fair use, but this is a complex legal area with significant risk.

Bottom line: do NOT release a commercial track using an AI clone of a recognizable singer's voice. The legal, financial, and reputational risks are severe.

Should I form an LLC for my music career? What about an S-Corp? I'm making about $50k from music now. [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

At $50k in music income, forming a business entity starts making real financial and legal sense. Here's the breakdown:

Sole Proprietor (what you are now by default): No formation cost. No liability protection — if someone sues your 'business,' your personal assets (car, savings) are at risk. All income is subject to self-employment tax (15.3%). Filing: Schedule C on your personal return.

Single-Member LLC: Formation cost: $50-$500 depending on your state (California is expensive at $800/year minimum franchise tax). Provides liability protection — a wall between your business and personal assets. Tax treatment: by default, taxed the same as sole proprietor (pass-through to your personal return). You still pay 15.3% SE tax on all profit. BUT: you can elect S-Corp taxation.

S-Corp Election (LLC taxed as S-Corp): This is where the tax savings kick in at $50k+. How it works: You pay yourself a 'reasonable salary' (say $35k) and take the remaining $15k as a distribution. You only pay SE tax (15.3%) on the $35k salary, NOT on the $15k distribution. Tax savings: ~$2,295/year (15.3% x $15k). Additional costs: payroll service ($50-$100/month), annual S-Corp tax return ($500-$1,500 for a CPA). Net savings after costs: ~$1,000-$1,500/year at $50k income. The savings grow as income increases.

When each makes sense: Under $30k music income: stay sole proprietor, focus on growing revenue. $30k-$50k: form an LLC for liability protection. Over $50k: LLC with S-Corp election for tax savings. Over $100k: S-Corp is a no-brainer, saving $5,000-$10,000+/year in SE tax.

Practical steps to form an LLC: (1) Choose a name (e.g., '[Your Stage Name] Music LLC'). (2) File Articles of Organization with your state's Secretary of State. (3) Get an EIN (free from IRS.gov). (4) Open a business bank account. (5) If electing S-Corp: file IRS Form 2553 within 75 days of your desired effective date.

A brand offered me $10,000 to use my song in a commercial. But they want a worldwide exclusive buyout in perpetuity. Should I take it? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"indie"}}]

$10,000 sounds good until you read 'worldwide exclusive in perpetuity' — that phrase should stop you cold.

**What you'd be agreeing to:**
You can NEVER license this song to anyone else, anywhere in the world, for any purpose, for the rest of your life (and possibly beyond, depending on language). No other commercials. No films. No TV placements. No other brands. Ever. One song permanently off the table for $10k.

**Is $10k fair for a worldwide exclusive in perpetuity?** It depends on the brand's reach, but as a baseline: no. Tier 1 brand perpetuity buyouts typically start at $50k-$250k+ for known songs. $10k for a perpetual worldwide exclusive is significantly underpriced unless this is a very small regional brand with limited distribution.

**What to counter-propose:**
- Term-limited license: $10k for 2 years in North America, non-exclusive. After 2 years, rights revert.
- Or: $10k non-exclusive worldwide for 5 years. You can still license to non-competing brands.
- Or: Keep the perpetuity but make it non-exclusive and raise the fee to $35-50k

**The advocate position:** You own this song. The goal is to monetize it multiple times across your career, not once. A perpetual exclusive is a career asset permanently removed from your catalog. Counter the offer — they came to you because they want YOUR song. If they walk on a reasonable counter, another brand will come.

Me and two friends wrote a song together at a writing camp. Nobody talked about splits. The song is blowing up on TikTok and now one of them is saying they deserve 50%. What do I do? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

This is the number one dispute in independent music and it's almost always preventable.

**The Legal Default:** Under U.S. copyright law (17 U.S.C. 101), when two or more people create a joint work with the intention of merging their contributions, each co-author has an EQUAL and UNDIVIDED interest. With three co-writers and no written agreement, the legal default is 33.3% each. Your friend claiming 50% has no legal basis unless you agreed to that split.

**Immediate Actions:**
1. Don't panic. The song blowing up is a good problem.
2. Document everything NOW — texts, DMs, session files, DAW projects, voice memos showing who contributed what.
3. Have an honest conversation before lawyers. An equal 33/33/33 is often the path of least resistance.
4. Get it in writing — use a split sheet (Splits.io, Splice's split sheet, or a simple Google Doc). Include: song title, legal names, percentages, PRO affiliations, signatures.
5. Each writer registers their share with their own PRO.

**If they won't negotiate:** Consult an entertainment attorney. Many offer free 30-minute consultations. Mediation ($500-$2,000) is far cheaper than litigation.

**The lesson:** Split sheets BEFORE you leave the studio. Always. Takes 5 minutes and prevents months of pain.

How can I avoid paying taxes on my streaming royalties? Can I route payments through an offshore account or claim my music income is a hobby? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

I cannot and will not advise on tax evasion strategies. Intentionally hiding income from the IRS is a federal crime with penalties including fines up to $250,000 and up to 5 years in prison.

To be clear: Routing payments offshore to avoid reporting is tax evasion (illegal). The IRS has international information-sharing agreements and tracks foreign accounts via FBAR and FATCA. Falsely claiming music income as a 'hobby' when you're actively trying to profit is fraud. The IRS uses a multi-factor test to determine if an activity is a business or hobby, and if they reclassify you, you'll owe back taxes plus penalties.

What I CAN help with — legal tax minimization: (1) Maximize legitimate deductions (equipment, travel, home studio, marketing). (2) Form an LLC with S-Corp election to reduce self-employment tax. (3) Contribute to a SEP-IRA or Solo 401(k) to defer income. (4) Time income and expenses across tax years strategically. (5) Work with a CPA who specializes in entertainment.

Would you like me to help you find legitimate ways to reduce your tax burden?

A producer sent me a beat lease agreement. What's the difference between exclusive and non-exclusive? Should I get the exclusive? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"hip-hop"}}]

This is one of the most important decisions you'll make on a budget, so let's get it right.

**Non-exclusive lease (typically $20-$200):** You can use the beat. So can 50 other artists. The producer keeps selling it. Your release will usually have restrictions — often capped at 10,000-50,000 streams, limited distribution platforms, no TV/sync licensing rights, and the producer retains the master split. When you hit the stream cap, you either upgrade or you're technically in breach of the lease.

**Exclusive rights ($200-$5,000+):** You and only you can use this beat commercially. The producer stops selling it. No stream cap. Typically includes broader distribution rights. You usually get more favorable master ownership terms.

**The math you need to do:** If you genuinely believe this song could do real numbers — if you're planning a push on it, putting sync money behind it, pitching it to playlists — then the non-exclusive lease is a liability. At 50k streams the lease expires. At 500k streams you're in violation. At a sync placement, you may not even have the rights to license it.

**Advocate take:** If you're making content to post online and learn your craft, a non-exclusive lease is fine. If you're building a catalog you plan to monetize, license, or build a career on: pay for the exclusive or move on to a beat you can own outright. Don't build your brand on a foundation you don't own.
